metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py:_resolve_tracker_dir` as a required update site and either place the `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` tombstone check in `tracker_dir_override()` (so all callers benefit) or add an explicit check at the reconciler's own entry point.
findings.finding: The plan places the `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` tombstone check at the top of `load_config()`, but `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py:_resolve_tracker_dir` (line 171-182) calls `tracker_dir_override()` directly — which only reads `os.environ.get('REBAR_TRACKER_DIR')` — and never calls `load_config()`. An operator running the reconciler with `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` set would bypass the tombstone entirely; the check needs to also fire at `tracker_dir_override()` or `tracker_dir()`, or the reconciler's direct call path must be enumerated as a required update site.
findings.location: What section / Scope (`src/rebar/config.py`) / `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py:171-182`
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` tombstone check into `tracker_dir_override()` itself (or at the top of `tracker_dir()`), not just `load_config()`. Since `tracker_dir_override()` is the single source for the env read, this covers all call paths — `load_config()`, `tracker_dir()`, and `_resolve_tracker_dir` — without additional per-site changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding table-driven test case) for `scan_tombstones` / `rebar config validate` with at least two simultaneously-set tombstoned inputs (one env, one TOML key) asserting that ALL matches appear in the output and none raises early.
findings.finding: The plan specifies only single-input test cases for `rebar config validate` / `scan_tombstones()`; no test verifies the critical ALL-matches behavior when multiple tombstoned inputs are simultaneously present — the key design differentiator over the raising `load_config()` path.
findings.location: AC: `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` bullet; Scope: `scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths) -> list[tuple[RemovedInput, str]]`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `rebar config validate` AC: 'When TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR and verify.require_verdict_for_close are both set, `rebar config validate` exits non-zero and the output contains both the TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR and verify.require_verdict_for_close tombstone messages (verifying scan_tombstones does not abort on the first match).' Mirror this with a pytest parametrize or fixture that sets multiple inputs and asserts len(results) >= 2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's reference from 'except Exception blocks at mcp_server.py:302-312/:311' to the single block 'mcp_server.py:302-312' to avoid confusion.
findings.finding: The plan refers to `mcp_server.py:302-312` and `mcp_server.py:311` as two separate `except Exception` blocks requiring `except RemovedInputError: raise` guards, but inspecting the file shows these are one single block (line 311 is inside the 302-312 block). There is only one `except Exception` in `mcp_server.py`. Treating them as two distinct locations will confuse the implementer and may lead to a double-guard on the same block.
findings.location: What section / Implementation sequencing (Slice 1) / `src/rebar/mcp_server.py:302-312`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`mcp_server.py:302-312`/`:311`' with '`mcp_server.py:302-312` (a single block)' throughout the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the emit-frequency policy for `warn`-class tombstones (e.g., emit at most once per process using a module-level `_warned: set[str]` sentinel, or delegate to `warnings.warn` with its built-in dedup) so the MCP server does not spam logs on every config access.
findings.finding: The emit-frequency policy for `warn`-class tombstones is unspecified: the plan places env-tombstone checks 'at the top of load_config() before the cache lookup' so they run on every call (including warm-cache returns in the long-running MCP server), but gives no dedup/once-per-process rule — warn-class tombstones will spam the `rebar` logger on every MCP request that touches config.
findings.location: What section — 'Cache-safe placement (important)'; AC line — 'Operationally-inert retired inputs warn rather than error — verified by...caplog'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `warnings.warn(msg, DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=...)` for warn-class tombstones; Python's default `warnings` filter deduplicates per (message, category, module, lineno) automatically.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the schema of `tests/golden/config_surface.json` (e.g., `{"config_keys": ["section.key", ...], "env_vars": ["REBAR_...", ...], "tombstones": ["env:NAME", ...], "aliases": ["env:NAME", ...]}`) so the anti-regression pytest is unambiguously implementable.
findings.finding: The schema for the committed `tests/golden/config_surface.json` golden file is never defined: the plan says the anti-regression test 'imports the live allowed-key set + env vars and the tombstone/alias registries, diffs against a committed config_surface.json', but does not specify what keys, structure, or format the golden file uses — making the test non-reproducible.
findings.location: Scope section — 'tests/golden/config_surface.json (committed) + a pytest...'; AC line — 'A CI/anti-regression check fails if a recognised config key / env var is removed without a corresponding tombstone entry'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify whether `tests/golden/config_surface.json` includes the `rebar.llm` env-var surface (e.g. `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`, `REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS`) and whether the CI pytest imports `LLMConfig`'s recognised env-var set alongside `_config_schema` — so the anti-regression guard covers the LLM layer boundary it already identified as a separate tombstone site.
findings.finding: The CI anti-regression golden-file check (tests/golden/config_surface.json) protects against future silent removals only for config keys/env vars that flow through `_config_schema` and `_deprecations.py`. However, the plan carves out `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` as living in `rebar.llm/LLMConfig.from_env()` — a separate layer not covered by the golden-file diff against `_config_schema`. The plan says 'its tombstone is applied in the LLM config load path' but does not specify whether the golden file includes LLM env vars, nor whether the CI check imports the LLM layer's key set. If not, future LLM env var removals are silently unguarded — the anti-regression guard has a known gap at the boundary it already identified.
findings.location: Scope section — 'src/rebar/llm/ (LLMConfig.from_env()) — apply the REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS tombstone there' and 'tests/golden/config_surface.json … imports the live allowed-key set (_config_schema's allowed keys) + env vars'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the golden-file CI check to also import and enumerate the LLM layer's recognised env var set (from `LLMConfig.from_env()` or a companion `_LLM_ENV_VARS` constant), include those names in `config_surface.json`, and fail if any LLM env var disappears without a tombstone in the LLM tombstone registry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign the static `mcp_server.py` guard-audit pytest explicitly to Slice 1 (alongside the re-raise code change) so the invariant is machine-checked from the moment the guard lands, not deferred to Slice 2.
findings.finding: The implementation sequencing assigns the `mcp_server.py` code change (adding `except RemovedInputError: raise` guards) to Slice 1, but the AC-required static audit pytest — 'a pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it' — is not assigned to either slice, creating ambiguity about when CI coverage for that guard lands and whether Slice 1 is considered done without it.
findings.location: Implementation sequencing — Slice 1 bullet; AC bullet 6 (MCP criterion)
findings.suggested_fix: Move the 'pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and asserts every `except (Exception|BaseException)` line has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it' from unassigned status into the Slice 1 bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the mechanical grep AC check to also assert that no bare `except:` clause appears in the scanned files without an immediately-preceding `except RemovedInputError: raise` guard (i.e., add `except:` to the regex alternation or run a second assertion), so the anti-swallow guarantee covers all handler forms the prose promises to audit.
findings.finding: The AC's mechanical grep check for anti-swallow verification uses the pattern `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` but this regex misses bare `except:` clauses. The plan's prose explicitly says to 'grep the config→CLI→MCP path to confirm no bare `except:` / `except BaseException` swallows it', but the only automated/reproducible check defined in the AC only covers the named-type forms. A bare `except:` anywhere in the MCP request-dispatch or framework layer would silently swallow `RemovedInputError` despite passing the AC check.
findings.location: AC line: 'Plus a pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it'
findings.suggested_fix: In the pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py`, extend the assertion to cover both patterns: `re.findall(r'except\s*(?:Exception|BaseException|:)', source)` — or more precisely, flag any `except` clause that is bare (`except:`) or catches `Exception`/`BaseException` and lacks an immediately-preceding `except RemovedInputError: raise`. Also extend the scan scope from just `mcp_server.py` to the full config→CLI→MCP call chain files listed in Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error state for `rebar config validate`/`doctor` when the project or user TOML file is syntactically malformed: define who is responsible for parsing TOML before handing `toml_tables` to `scan_tombstones`, and state whether a parse error is reported as a distinct diagnostic message (recommended) or causes the TOML-tombstone check to be skipped with a warning.
findings.finding: The `scan_tombstones()` function used by `rebar config validate`/`doctor` is specified to parse env + TOML + file paths directly (bypassing `load_config()`), but the plan does not specify what the command should do if the project TOML file is syntactically malformed (parse error, not a tombstone match). A diagnostic command encountering an unparseable config should report a distinct, actionable error rather than crash with a raw traceback or silently skip TOML-key tombstone checks — but neither behavior is specified.
findings.location: Scope: 'New CLI subcommand (config validate / doctor)' and AC: 'rebar config validate / rebar doctor uses scan_tombstones(...)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/AC for the doctor command: 'If TOML parsing fails, `config validate` catches the parse exception, prints a targeted message (file path + parse error), counts it as a non-tombstone hard error, and exits non-zero — it does not propagate a raw traceback. TOML-key tombstone checks are skipped for the unparseable file but env-var and file-presence checks still run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the canonical module that exports `scan_tombstones` (e.g., `_deprecations.py` alongside the `RemovedInput` registry) so all callers — `config validate`, AC tests, and the anti-regression guard — share one import path.
findings.finding: The `scan_tombstones` function's definitive module home is never specified; the plan says it is wired from 'show_config.py' but also describes it as a standalone function that the validate/doctor command and AC tests import directly — leaving implementers to guess whether it lives in `_deprecations.py`, `show_config.py`, a new `_tombstones.py`, or `config.py`.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New CLI subcommand (config validate / doctor) + wiring (show_config.py), plus a scan_tombstones(...) function'; AC line 'rebar config validate / rebar doctor uses scan_tombstones(...)'
findings.suggested_fix: Declare `scan_tombstones` in `_deprecations.py` (co-located with the `RemovedInput` registry); `show_config.py` imports and calls it rather than owning it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out mechanism (e.g., a `REBAR_TOMBSTONE_BEHAVIOR=warn` override or a documented expand-contract rollout sequence — warn in vN, error in vN+1) so operators with stale manifests have a non-blocking path to migrate before the hard-error takes effect.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a hard-error behavioral break (silent-ignore → `RemovedInputError`) for operators with stale deployment manifests containing retired env vars or TOML keys, but provides no rollback/back-out path or temporary suppression mechanism. T4 requires an explicit rollback or expand-contract sequencing — explicit justification of the break and a migration message are necessary but insufficient.
findings.location: What section — 'Distinct, un-swallowable error type' bullet; Scope — `config.py`; AC bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `REBAR_TOMBSTONE_BEHAVIOR=warn|error` environment variable (default `error` for security-class, `warn` for inert-class) that lets operators temporarily downgrade error-class tombstones to warnings. Document the override as a short-lived migration aid with a sunset version. Alternatively, adopt an explicit expand-contract sequence: introduce tombstones as warnings in the current release, promote to errors in the next minor release, and document this in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies `_TOMBSTONE_KINDS == frozenset({'env','cfg','file'})` and `_TOMBSTONE_BEHAVIORS == frozenset({'error','warn'})` are defined separately from the existing `_KINDS` registry — e.g., 'a unit test (`test_deprecation_registry`) asserts the two vocabulary sets exist, are the expected frozensets, and share no elements with `_KINDS`.'
findings.finding: The Scope section describes updating `test_deprecation_registry` to cover the new `_TOMBSTONE_KINDS` and `_TOMBSTONE_BEHAVIORS` vocabulary constants, but no Acceptance Criterion covers this test update — leaving the vocabulary-constant contract untested per the AC gate.
findings.location: Scope section — '`src/rebar/_deprecations.py` — … update `test_deprecation_registry` for the new vocabularies.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the `RemovedInputError(BaseException)` design rationale (specifically: why BaseException, not Exception or ConfigError, and which catch-sites it must escape) in a durable artifact — either a new ADR, a CLAUDE.md entry, or a prominent module-level comment block in `_deprecations.py` — so the invariant survives the plan document.
findings.finding: The `RemovedInputError(BaseException)` inheritance is explicitly called the 'load-bearing choice' that prevents silent swallowing — but the plan does not call for capturing this rationale in any persistent artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md, design comment in _deprecations.py). A future developer encountering the class and 'fixing' it to subclass Exception or ConfigError would re-introduce the exact silent-redirect bug the design prevents.
findings.location: What section ('Distinct, un-swallowable error type'); Scope section ('src/rebar/_deprecations.py'); no ADR/CLAUDE.md update mentioned
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Add a module-level docstring block in `_deprecations.py` (or a new `docs/adr/tombstone-basexception.md`) explaining that `RemovedInputError` MUST subclass `BaseException`, naming the two swallow-sites it must escape (`tracker_dir()` except ConfigError, `mcp_server.py:302-312` except Exception), and stating that any future change to the inheritance hierarchy must audit those sites first.' Also update CLAUDE.md or equivalent developer guide with a one-liner cross-reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `pytest-socket` from AC2 and specify only the `unittest.mock.patch` approach, which is already used in this codebase — or explicitly add `pytest-socket` to the project's test dependencies and confirm it is vetted.
findings.finding: The plan's AC2 proposes `pytest-socket` as a network-isolation mechanism for the table-driven test, but `pytest-socket` has no evidence of being an installed or declared dependency in this project. Introducing it adds a new optional test dependency with no existing call-site, potentially without the project's standard dependency-vetting process.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('enforced via `pytest-socket`, or a `unittest.mock.patch`...')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'enforced via `pytest-socket`, or a `unittest.mock.patch`…' with 'enforced via `unittest.mock.patch` on the lowest-level HTTP/socket primitive asserting `call_count == 0`' (drop the `pytest-socket` option since it has no existing footprint).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the TOML-key warm-cache gap: the plan's 'inherently safe' claim relies solely on file-token invalidation, which is vulnerable to same-second in-place edits on coarse-mtime filesystems in a long-running MCP server — either add tombstoned TOML key names to the cache-key signature (analogous to how env-var tombstones are added to `_env_signature()`), or move the TOML-key tombstone check to the top of `load_config()` alongside the env-var check (after parsing the TOML but before the cache lookup).
findings.finding: The TOML-key warm-cache safety argument has a hidden ordering assumption that can be violated in the long-running MCP server: the plan claims 'a warm entry can only exist for a config that was clean at population time' and that a changed config file triggers a cache miss via `_file_token` mismatch. However, the tombstone check inside `_resolve()` fires *before* writing to `_RESULT_CACHE`, but the cache invalidation logic (`_file_token` mismatch) depends on the file having actually changed on disk. If an operator adds a tombstoned TOML key to an *already-tracked and unchanged* config file (e.g. by editing in place in a way that preserves mtime, or on a filesystem with 1-second mtime granularity), the file token may not change within the same second, and the warm cache entry stays, silently bypassing the TOML-key tombstone. This is a TOCTOU-class gap: the plan's 'inherently warm-cache-safe' claim is true for the initial load but not for a running MCP server that holds the cache across an in-place config edit.
findings.location: What section — 'TOML-key tombstones … are checked inside _resolve() … inherently warm-cache-safe with no cache-structure change'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_toml_tombstone_signature(toml_tables)` step that hashes whether any tombstoned TOML key is present in the parsed tables, and include it in the cache key — mirroring the env-var approach. Alternatively, move the TOML tombstone check to a pre-cache position in `load_config()` by doing a lightweight key-presence scan before the `_RESULT_CACHE` lookup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the persistent AST-based pytest guard (currently scoped to `mcp_server.py`) to cover the entire config→CLI→MCP call path — at minimum `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` and `src/rebar/config.py` — asserting no bare `except:` or `except BaseException` block exists that does not explicitly re-raise `RemovedInputError`, so the BaseException propagation invariant is machine-checked continuously, not just at audit time.
findings.finding: The `except RemovedInputError: raise` guard-before-broad-catch approach in mcp_server.py is specified only for the two known sites (`:302-312`/`:311`), and the plan adds a pytest that asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a re-raise guard immediately above it. But the plan also notes the MCP boot ensure-sweep wraps `_config.tracker_dir()` in broad `except Exception` — meaning new broad-catch sites added in future (or existing ones missed by the regex) would silently swallow `RemovedInputError` again. The `BaseException` subclassing is the primary defense, but the plan's structural argument for why `BaseException` is not caught anywhere relies on there being *no* `except BaseException` or bare `except:` in the MCP/CLI path. The plan says 'Grep the config→CLI→MCP path to confirm no bare `except:` / `except BaseException` swallows it' — but this is specified as a grep/audit step, not as a persistent automated test. If a bare `except:` or `except BaseException` exists today or is added later, the `BaseException` defense fails and the plan's load-bearing assumption breaks silently.
findings.location: What section — 'Defensively re-raise at the MCP boot handlers' and AC — 'a pytest that reads src/rebar/mcp_server.py and asserts every line matching except\s+(Exception|BaseException) has a RemovedInputError: raise guard immediately above it'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC pytest that checks `mcp_server.py`, extend `paths_to_check` to `[mcp_server.py, _cli/__init__.py, config.py]` and use `ast.parse` to walk all `ExceptHandler` nodes, flagging any that catch `BaseException` or have no type (bare `except:`) without a preceding `RemovedInputError` re-raise guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove the `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` subcommand and `scan_tombstones` from this leaf's scope (confine Finding 5 to the raising fail-loud path the parent authorises), or update the parent epic (2610-9557-c3c1-460c) to explicitly include a diagnostic subcommand before landing this leaf.
findings.finding: The leaf introduces a new `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` CLI subcommand that does not appear anywhere in the parent epic's What, Success Criteria, or Acceptance Criteria. The parent's entire contract for Finding 5 is 'fail loud with a targeted migration message and a nonzero exit'; a dedicated scanning subcommand is additional scope the parent does not authorise.
findings.location: Leaf What section ('Add a `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command…'); Leaf Scope ('New CLI subcommand (`config validate` / `doctor`) + wiring (`show_config.py`), plus a `scan_tombstones(…)` function'); Leaf AC ('`rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` uses `scan_tombstones(…)`…')
findings.suggested_fix: The cleanest resolution is to update the parent epic's Finding 5 success criterion to read: '…fail loud … nonzero exit; a `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command reports all tombstone/alias matches without raising, for use in CI and deployment checks; genuinely unknown keys keep the existing forward-compatibility policy.' Then re-attest the parent before merging the leaf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove the `tests/golden/config_surface.json` golden-file anti-regression guard from this leaf's scope, or update the parent epic (2610-9557-c3c1-460c) to explicitly authorise a CI golden-list mechanism for config-key coverage before landing this leaf.
findings.finding: The leaf introduces a CI anti-regression golden-file guard (`tests/golden/config_surface.json` + a pytest diff against the live allowed-key set) that is entirely absent from the parent epic's plan, Success Criteria, and Acceptance Criteria. The parent says nothing about a committed golden list or a CI check that diffs keys against it.
findings.location: Leaf What section ('Prevent future silent removals (anti-regression guard)… Add a CI check that compares the set of recognised config keys / env vars against a committed golden list…'); Leaf Scope ('`tests/golden/config_surface.json` (committed) + a pytest that imports the live allowed-key set…'); Leaf AC ('A CI/anti-regression check fails if a recognised config key / env var is removed without a corresponding tombstone entry…')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `pytest-socket` dependency question: either add it to `pyproject.toml` dev deps and confirm it is available in CI, or remove it from the AC and replace with a concrete `unittest.mock.patch` target (e.g. `socket.socket.__init__`) so the network-isolation assertion is unambiguously implementable without an uninstalled package.
findings.finding: `pytest-socket` is referenced in an AC as the network-isolation enforcement mechanism but is not listed in pyproject.toml dev dependencies and does not appear anywhere in the codebase — its availability is unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — second bullet: 'no network call (enforced via pytest-socket, or a unittest.mock.patch…)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'enforced via pytest-socket, or a unittest.mock.patch' with a concrete mock-patch approach: `unittest.mock.patch('socket.socket', side_effect=RuntimeError('network calls forbidden'))` wrapping only the config-load call under test, since pytest-socket is not an existing project dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether extending the existing `Deprecation` dataclass with `behavior: Literal['error','warn'] = 'warn'` and `removed: bool = False` would suffice, or document in the plan why a fully separate `RemovedInput` dataclass (rather than a subclass or extended field) is necessary to avoid coupling the two registries' consumers.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new parallel `RemovedInput` dataclass alongside the existing `Deprecation` dataclass in `_deprecations.py`, rather than extending `Deprecation` with a `behavior: Literal['error','warn']` field and a `removed: bool` flag. The existing `Deprecation` already has `kind`, `name`, `replacement`, `remove_in`, `permanent` — all the structure a tombstone needs except `behavior`. Two co-resident dataclasses in the same module with overlapping fields creates a conceptual split that future contributors must navigate.
findings.location: What section — 'Add a `RemovedInput` tombstone registry … (separate from the existing alias-registry `_KINDS`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `rebar doctor` is a top-level subcommand (requiring a dispatch arm in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` and a `known_subcommands()` entry in `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py`) or an alias for `rebar config validate` (no separate wiring needed), and add the missing file targets to the Scope if it is top-level.
findings.finding: The plan names `rebar config validate` and `rebar doctor` as two invocation forms for the same command, but only identifies `show_config.py` as the edit target for wiring. If `rebar doctor` is meant as a separate top-level subcommand, it requires an additional dispatch arm in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (an intercept block) and a `known_subcommands()` registration in `src/rebar/_cli/_help.py` — neither of which are listed in the Scope. If it is purely an alias routed through the `config` intercept (e.g. `rebar config validate`), the plan should drop the top-level `rebar doctor` form to avoid the ambiguity.
findings.location: Scope / What section / AC ('rebar config validate / rebar doctor')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the grep-and-audit scope in the plan to explicitly include `src/rebar/_engine_support/` and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/` `except ConfigError` call sites, confirming that `RemovedInputError` propagates correctly (or is intentionally caught) at each.
findings.finding: Multiple `except ConfigError` call sites outside the MCP path — in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/` (`outbound_differ.py:647`, `rebar_id_audit.py:158`, `run_differs.py:660`, `acli_subprocess.py:62,94`, `_advisory_lock.py:70,87`) and `src/rebar/_engine_support/` (`gates.py:154`, `lookups.py:74`, `reads.py:118,182,300`) — all call `load_config()` and would let a `BaseException`-subclass `RemovedInputError` propagate unchecked. The plan's audit instruction ('Grep the config→CLI→MCP path for no bare except: / except BaseException') does not enumerate these as explicit audit sites, and the AC's 'belt-and-suspenders' re-raise guard is scoped only to `mcp_server.py`. In the reconciler sub-process, an unguarded `RemovedInputError` propagation is desirable (hard fail), but in the CLI's `_engine_support` path a `BaseException` propagating past the CLI's `try/except RemovedInputError` wrapper would be a traceback rather than a clean exit if the call happens in a subprocess or worker rather than the CLI's `main()` frame.
findings.location: What section ('Defensively re-raise') / Scope (MCP server entry path) / AC (MCP tombstone test)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the propagation audit and grep-assertion test beyond `mcp_server.py` to cover at minimum `llm/config.py`, `llm/gate_source.py`, and `llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` — the three identified sites with broad `except Exception` blocks that wrap `load_config()` / `tracker_dir()` — and either add `except RemovedInputError: raise` guards there or document why those paths cannot trigger a RemovedInputError mid-request (e.g. they only run after config is already successfully cached).
findings.finding: The propagation-guarantee test scope (the AC's grep-assertion) only covers `mcp_server.py`, but multiple other files with broad `except Exception` blocks can catch `load_config()`-triggered `RemovedInputError` — notably `llm/config.py:373` which wraps `tracker_dir()` directly and is outside the mcp_server.py scan.
findings.location: What section — 'Defensively re-raise at the MCP boot handlers' + AC bullet: 'a pytest that reads src/rebar/mcp_server.py and asserts every line matching except\s+(Exception|BaseException) has a RemovedInputError: raise guard immediately above it'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either drop `pytest-socket` from AC2 and commit to the stdlib `unittest.mock.patch` fallback exclusively, OR add `pytest-socket` to `pyproject.toml` `[dev]` dependencies and verify it installs cleanly with the rest of the dev stack before spec-ing it as the primary mechanism.
findings.finding: `pytest-socket` is named in AC2 as a network-isolation mechanism but is not declared in pyproject.toml [dev] dependencies and is not installed in the project environment — it is an unverified external test dependency. The plan does name a stdlib fallback (`unittest.mock.patch` on the lowest-level HTTP/socket primitive asserting `call_count == 0`), but the primary mechanism is unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'Setting a store/security/policy/sync-affecting retired input … does not mutate the store or reach the network — verified … (enforced via `pytest-socket`, or a `unittest.mock.patch` on the lowest-level HTTP/socket primitive asserting `call_count == 0`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, replace the `pytest-socket` option with the already-specified fallback: '…and no network call (enforced via `unittest.mock.patch` on the lowest-level HTTP/socket primitive, asserting `call_count == 0`)'. This removes the undeclared dependency without weakening the guarantee.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly constrain all tombstone error/warning message formatters (both `RemovedInputError.__str__` and the `warn`-path logger call) to include ONLY the key name, replacement, and removed-in metadata — never the runtime value of the retired input — and add an AC assertion that the message text does not contain the test value used to set the retired var.
findings.finding: The error message and warning text for tombstoned inputs is specified to contain 'old name + replacement + removed-in release + a concise migration example' but is never explicitly constrained to exclude the runtime VALUE of the retired input. On the MCP-server path (a persistent network-accessible process whose stderr/logs may be captured by an orchestrator or log aggregator), if an operator stores a secret in a renamed env var (e.g. an API token in `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` or a path containing credentials in `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`) and the tombstone message interpolates the value into the error string, the secret would be emitted to stderr and any downstream log sink — crossing the boundary from the process environment to an external observer.
findings.location: What section — 'Hard-error … message naming the old name, replacement, removed-in release, and a concise migration example'; also AC line: 'fails with a nonzero exit and a message naming the old name, the replacement, and the removed-in release'; Scope — `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:main()` and `mcp_server.py` error path
findings.suggested_fix: In the `RemovedInput` message-formatting code (wherever `str(e)` is assembled), build the message strictly as f"Removed input {self.name!r} (removed in {self.removed_in}): use {self.replacement!r} instead." with no reference to `os.environ[self.name]` or any user-supplied value; add a test assertion `assert TEST_SECRET_VALUE not in str(exc)` alongside the existing tombstone tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the handling protocol for the `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` warn tombstone when `LLMConfig.from_env()` is invoked inside degrade-to-defaults `except Exception` blocks in the agentic layer (confirm the warning is emitted before the exception path, or note that degrade-to-defaults is intentional even when the tombstone fires).
findings.finding: The `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` warn-class tombstone placed in `LLMConfig.from_env()` is called inside multiple broad `except Exception` handlers in the LLM layer (e.g., `llm/gate_source.py:54`, `llm/plan_review/__init__.py:78`, `llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:436`). A `warn`-class tombstone only emits a log warning (not a `BaseException`), so it is not swallowed — but the plan's stated audit 'grep the config→CLI→MCP path' does not mention these call sites, leaving the reader unable to verify the tombstone actually fires in all agentic entry points.
findings.location: What section — 'REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS→REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS lives in the optional rebar.llm layer'; Scope — 'src/rebar/llm/ (LLMConfig.from_env())'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
